proposals.name: Elaine Brown
proposals.role: BPP Chairperson 1974–77; "A Taste of Power" memoirist; 1973/75 Oakland City Council candidate
proposals.why_they_belong: Recurring Whisper failure ("Lane Brown," "Lane") across the transcript tail. BPP leadership figure of comparable canonical weight to David Hilliard (already in corpus). Appears in at least 6 distinct transcript passages covering the Oakland 1973 electoral campaign, the Lionel Wilson mayoral race, and the party's post-purge organizational period. Cross-corpus recurrence expected.
proposals.transcript_evidence: "Bobby said, you know, Lane Brown for City Council." (corrected: Elaine Brown); "I bought Elaine Brown up here on Thursday along with Cha Cha Jimenez" (Pass 2 tail 1.P2T.10, 1.P2T.47)

proposals.name: Bobby Rush
proposals.role: Illinois BPP co-founder; US Representative 1993–2023 (IL-1)
proposals.why_they_belong: Target of the December 4, 1969 FBI/Chicago PD raid that killed Fred Hampton ("they wanted to kill Bobby Rush, but he wasn't there"). Fred Hampton is already in corpus; Bobby Rush is the co-founder who survived and went on to a 30-year Congressional career. His absence from the corpus creates an asymmetry in the Hampton-related entries. Likely recurring in other Illinois BPP interview transcripts.
proposals.transcript_evidence: "They start in Chicago. They go in there. They kill Fred Hampton. And they wanted to kill Bobby Rush, but he wasn't there." (Pass 2 tail 1.P2T.9, marked `correct`)

proposals.name: Alprentice "Bunchy" Carter
proposals.role: Los Angeles BPP deputy minister of defense; assassinated UCLA Campbell Hall January 17, 1969
proposals.why_they_belong: Appears with John Huggins in the same assassination (1.22, 1.P2.13, both HIGH confidence after Pass 3 promotion). John Huggins' wife Ericka Huggins is a significant BPP figure in her own right (listed in Pass 3 corpus candidates). Having neither Carter nor the Hugginses in the corpus when the Campbell Hall assassination is one of the three canonical BPP martyrdom events (alongside Bobby Hutton and Fred Hampton, both already in corpus) is a significant gap. Cross-corpus recurrence high: any BPP interview is likely to reference the 1969 UCLA shootings.
proposals.transcript_evidence: "Bunchy Carter and John Huggins, the two leaders of the Southern California chapter are assassinated" (corrected from "Eldritch John Huggins"; Pass 1 row 1.22, Pass 2 row 1.P2.13, both HIGH)

proposals.name: Mordecai Wyatt Johnson
proposals.role: First Black president of Howard University (1926–1960); Crozer Theological Seminary alumnus; architect of Howard Law School's constitutional-law program through Charles Hamilton Houston; MLK's intellectual mentor via the Morehouse-Howard-Crozer chain
proposals.why_they_belong: Foundational figure for the entire Black-Protestant-to-civil-rights intellectual pipeline that Brown's testimony documents. Named in the SCLC entry ("Joseph Lowery") but not as a standalone figure. Multiple corpus entries (Thurgood Marshall, Charles Hamilton Houston, James Lawson) would point back to him. Any Morehouse/Crozer/Howard-alum interviewee will reference Johnson.
proposals.transcript_evidence: "That is why we got the blessing and the gift of Mordecai Wyatt Johnson. He too finished Morehouse College. And went to Colgate Rochester...Dr. Mordecai Wyatt Johnson, born in Paris, Tennessee...he was elected president in 1926. When he got there, his first agenda item was to discover what could be done to bring that law school to first class status... He called on Justice Louis Brandeis... he sought out Charles Hamilton Houston." Pass 4 row 2.P4.40 confirmed Ralph Bunche and Sterling Allen Brown as co-candidates; all are in the same passage.

proposals.name: Ralph Bunche
proposals.role: UN Under-Secretary-General; Nobel Peace Prize laureate (1950); first African American to win the Nobel Peace Prize; Howard University alumnus; foundational Black diplomat and internationalist. Named by Brown as one of the candidates passed over for the Howard presidency.
proposals.why_they_belong: Bunche is cross-corpus relevant — any interviewee discussing the UN, international civil rights, or Howard University will reference him. The 140-entry corpus has no standalone Bunche entry despite his canonical stature. The Thurgood Marshall entry mentions Howard but not Bunche.
proposals.transcript_evidence: "people like Ralph Bunch, Sterling Brown and others, they felt that they were scholars and they should have been considered [for the Howard presidency]." Pass 4 row 2.P4.40 confirmed canonical identification. Pass 2/3 missed the reference entirely; it first surfaced in Pass 4.

proposals.name: John Hope Franklin
proposals.role: Preeminent African American historian (1915–2009); Duke University professor; chaired President Clinton's 1997–98 "One America" race commission; author of From Slavery to Freedom (1947, the standard Black history textbook for decades)
proposals.why_they_belong: Any interview touching post-1947 Black historical consciousness or the Clinton era will reference Franklin. Brown names him explicitly in the context of an active civic event (2013 interview referencing the 1997 race commission). The corpus has no Franklin entry. His textbook shaped the generation of interviewees in this entire corpus.
proposals.transcript_evidence: "President Bill Clinton tried to do it with his commission on race. That Dr. John Hope Franklin was chair, but that commission was derailed." Pass 4 row 2.P4.39 confirmed canonical identification (Whisper rendered "John O. Franklin"; corrected to "John Hope Franklin").

proposals.name: Amelia Boynton Robinson
proposals.role: Selma voting-rights leader; beaten unconscious on Bloody Sunday at Edmund Pettus Bridge; her photographed collapse became an iconic Movement image
proposals.why_they_belong: One of the most frequently-named Selma figures across all Selma-veteran transcripts; canonical historical figure documented in every Bloody Sunday account; NOT in 140-entry corpus despite direct transcript mention
proposals.transcript_evidence: Corrected transcript: "you'll see (Amelia) Boynton and Miss Marie Foster." Pass 1 row 3.56: "Amelia Boynton Robinson — Selma voting rights leader, beaten unconscious on Bloody Sunday." The bracketed correction appears in the corrected transcript confirming canonical identification.

proposals.name: Ruby Doris Smith Robinson
proposals.role: SNCC Executive Secretary 1966–67; died October 1967 at age 25; among the most powerful women in SNCC's organizational history
proposals.why_they_belong: Catalog row C records "Rubidorus Robinson → Ruby Doris Smith Robinson" as a corpus-wide recurring pattern (entries #32, #44 among others); Avery names her multiple times as a close colleague; NOT in 140-entry corpus
proposals.transcript_evidence: Corrected transcript: "I met Ruby Doris Smith." Pass 2 row 3.P2.7 (high): "Ruby Doris Smith Robinson — canonical SNCC Executive Secretary (1966-67); died Oct 1967." Multiple occurrences across the corpus make this a high-priority addition.

proposals.name: Prathia Hall (Rev. Prathia Hall Wynn)
proposals.role: SNCC Southwest Georgia organizer; AME theologian at Boston University; her prayer phrase "I have a dream" at Mount Olive Baptist Church in Terrell County, GA (1962) is documented as an influence on King's August 1963 speech
proposals.why_they_belong: Avery names her as a close SNCC colleague; Hall's documented role in the Albany Movement and her influence on King's most famous speech make her a high canonical value; NOT in 140-entry corpus
proposals.transcript_evidence: Corrected transcript: "Rev. Prathia Hall." Pass 1 row 3.38 (high), Pass 2 row 3.P2.11 (high): "Canonical SNCC SW GA + later Boston U Theology faculty + AME minister." Multiple confirmations across passes.

4.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer

proposals.name: Dr. Robert B. Hayling
proposals.role: Dentist; founder of the modern St. Augustine civil rights movement; NAACP Youth Council advisor; recruited MLK to St. Augustine; Air Force dental officer. Central figure of the entire 1963–64 St. Augustine campaign.
proposals.why_they_belong: This is the single highest-priority corpus addition from the entire St. Augustine sub-corpus. Hayling appears in nearly every St. Augustine entry across Passes 1–4 under multiple Whisper-mangled forms (Dr. Haillin, Dr. Helen, Dr. Harvey Haley, Dr. Arby Helen, Dr. R.B. Hayling, Dr. Cain/Ken/Martha Luther King misattributions). The scorer cannot reliably ground accuracy claims about the St. Augustine campaign without this entry. Not currently in civil_rights_facts.json (confirmed by Pass 7 corpus check).
proposals.transcript_evidence: Corrected transcript: named throughout as "Dr. Robert B. Hayling"; direct-address, third-person reference, and biographical detail abundant. Whisper variants indexed in rows 4.2, 4.P2.1, 4.P4.1, 4.P4.36.

proposals.name: The St. Augustine Four
proposals.role: Collective: Audrey Nell Edwards Hamilton, JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer, Samuel White (d. 1995), Willie Carl Singleton — four Black teenagers sentenced to Florida reform schools in 1963 for refusing to renounce sit-in demonstrations.
proposals.why_they_belong: The canonical collective designation for the four interviewees + their male co-defendants. Without this entry the scorer cannot recognize "St. Augustine Four" as a known historical referent and may flag correct summary claims as unsupported. Appears as "Santa Augustine fall," "Synogostean for," "St. Augustine Four" in the corrected transcript — three distinct Whisper forms of the same canonical referent.
proposals.transcript_evidence: Corrected transcript: "Not just the St. Augustine Four, but the whole movement." Pass 4 row 4.P4.32 confirms. Pass 1 rows 4.19–4.20 establish all four member names.

proposals.name: Sheriff L.O. Davis (Lawrence O. Davis)
proposals.role: St. Johns County, FL Sheriff 1948–1972; documented Klan collaborator; oversaw the 1963–64 mass arrests including the St. Augustine Four; present at the McCrory's/Woolworth's arrests.
proposals.why_they_belong: Davis is the primary law-enforcement antagonist of the St. Augustine campaign and appears across the corrected transcript as "L.O. Davis," "Elo Davis," and "Ella Davis" — Whisper variants that a scorer would not recognize as the same canonical figure. His Klan ties and the documented pattern of mass arrest are essential accuracy grounding for summaries of the St. Augustine entries. Not in corpus (confirmed).
proposals.transcript_evidence: Corrected transcript: "they called in L.O. Davis, which was the sheriff" — speaker provides the canonical identification directly. Pass 1 row 4.25, Pass 2 row 4.P2.7 confirm.

proposals.why_they_belong: Role
proposals.transcript_evidence: Why they belong

proposals.role: Hollis Watkins** (b. 1941)
proposals.why_they_belong: Foundational McComb / Mississippi SNCC field worker 1961–64; pioneered Pike County voter-registration with Curtis Hayes and Bob Moses; later Mississippi Freedom Schools organizer
proposals.transcript_evidence: McLaurin's testimony (SRT implied via row 17.P2T.30–31 + "Highless" Whisper variant) provides first-person attribution of Watkins as the canonical McComb-period figure. The "Highless" garble (row 17.P2T.30) is the primary Whisper failure pattern for his name in this corpus. His role as the first SNCC organizer arrested in Mississippi (McComb, 1961) is corpus-wide significant.

proposals.role: Lafayette Surney** (b. ~1944, Ruleville MS)
proposals.why_they_belong: SNCC field secretary recruited by McLaurin in Ruleville, August 1962; one of the original Delta organizers in the Sunflower County project; recent Ruleville High School graduate at recruitment
proposals.transcript_evidence: Foundational Ruleville-period figure named by McLaurin with first-person attribution. Not in corpus. Whisper renders as "Lafayette Sonny" / "Laffeyette Sonny." Surney's recruitment as a local teenager who knew the community gave SNCC its initial Ruleville foothold.

proposals.role: Isaiah T. Montgomery** (1847–1924)
proposals.why_they_belong: Founder of Mound Bayou, MS (1887); only Black delegate to the 1890 MS constitutional convention that disenfranchised Black voters — voted with the white delegation
proposals.transcript_evidence: McLaurin cites Montgomery to explain the historical context of Black political erasure in Mississippi. The Davis-Bend-to-Mound-Bayou founding narrative is foundational to understanding the Delta political geography McLaurin describes throughout the tail. Not in corpus.

proposals.role: Candidate
proposals.why_they_belong: Role
proposals.transcript_evidence: Transcript evidence

proposals.role: Cordell Reagon (1943–1996)
proposals.why_they_belong: SNCC field secretary + co-founder of SNCC Freedom Singers (with Bernice Johnson Reagon, his first wife); Albany Movement co-organizer with Sherrod and Charles Jones.
proposals.transcript_evidence: Transcript: "Charles A. Jones and Cordell Reagon and myself got the permission from the adults and the community to bring the movement into Albany, Georgia." Direct first-person co-organizer citation.

proposals.role: Friendship Nine
proposals.why_they_belong: The February 1961 Rock Hill, SC jail-no-bail action at Friendship Junior College; the canonical originating event of SNCC's "jail-no-bail" tactic that Sherrod then carried to Albany.
proposals.transcript_evidence: Transcript: "Rockill South Carolina ... after coming from Rockill ... jailed there on the chain gang" — oblique reference confirmed by corpus. The Albany Movement corpus entry does not mention Friendship Nine; it is a distinct foundational event.

proposals.role: Laurie Pritchett (1925–2000)
proposals.why_they_belong: Albany police chief 1959–66; pioneer of the "nonviolent containment" counter-strategy (dispersing arrests across multiple county jails to deny the movement visible-brutality images) that is considered the canonical Albany Movement tactical failure.
proposals.transcript_evidence: Transcript: "the relative savvy of Laurie Pritchett and managing the distribute all the prisoners across these many county jails." Direct named citation with strategic description.

proposals.why_they_belong: Role
proposals.transcript_evidence: Why they belong

proposals.name: GT1
proposals.role: R. Jess Brown
proposals.why_they_belong: Pioneer Black Mississippi attorney; Medgar Evers's legal counsel; Clyde Kennard's habeas corpus attorney; one of only two or three Black attorneys practicing in Mississippi in the 1950s
proposals.transcript_evidence: Central to the Kennard habeas hearing that McGee witnessed; named explicitly in transcript; cross-references Medgar Evers corpus entry. Canonical figure for the MS civil rights legal infrastructure. Pass 3 + Pass 4 both propose this addition.

proposals.name: GT2
proposals.role: Samuel Holloway Bowers Jr. (Sam Bowers)
proposals.why_they_belong: Imperial Wizard of the White Knights of the KKK in Mississippi; convicted 1998 for ordering the murder of Vernon Dahmer Sr. (January 10, 1966); also implicated in the 1964 Mississippi Burning murders (Chaney/Goodman/Schwerner)
proposals.transcript_evidence: The 1998 conviction closes the arc of the longest-running KKK-murder prosecution in Mississippi history. Multiple entries reference Bowers; he appears in the Vernon Dahmer Sr. corpus entry's summary but deserves a standalone entry. Pass 3 + Pass 4 both propose this addition.

proposals.name: GT3
proposals.role: J.C. Fairley (Rev. J.C. Fairley)
proposals.why_they_belong: Hattiesburg NAACP president, predecessor to Dr. C.E. Smith; recurring figure in Hattiesburg-area transcripts; documented in Charles Payne's *I've Got the Light of Freedom* (1995)
proposals.transcript_evidence: Explicitly named as McGee's first NAACP connection through shared church membership. Pass 3 + Pass 4 both propose this addition. Recurring across Hattiesburg-area entries (#21, #25, likely others).

proposals.name: Samuel Younge Jr. (Sammy Younge

Candidate
proposals.why_they_belong: Role
proposals.transcript_evidence: Transcript evidence

proposals.name: GT.1
proposals.role: Z.T. Mathews (Sheriff Zebulon Thomas Mathews)
proposals.why_they_belong: Terrell County GA sheriff; canonical antagonist of the SW Georgia Movement; led the armed invasion of Mt. Olive Baptist Church mass meeting, summer 1962
proposals.transcript_evidence: Transcript: "ZT Matthews is what we call him. He was the sheriff's name." Speaker saw him in person at the Mt. Olive invasion. His "we want our colored people to live like they've been living for the last hundred years" quote is one of the most-cited segregationist statements.

proposals.name: GT.2
proposals.role: Reidsville State Prison (Georgia State Prison, Reidsville, Tattnall County, GA)
proposals.why_they_belong: Maximum-security Georgia state prison; site of a notable Dr. King detention (October 1960); later a location where Browner worked as an ex-offender program counselor
proposals.transcript_evidence: Transcript: "I had gotten the job to reach Reidsville State Prison recruited by the state of Georgia... It's east of here. Going towards Savannah." Pass 4 catch (#23.P4.1-2): "Reesfield prison" / "real estate prison" → Reidsville.

proposals.name: GT.3
proposals.role: "Terrible Terrell" County (Terrell County, GA — canonical SNCC shorthand)
proposals.why_they_belong: SNCC shorthand for Terrell County, GA — site of canonical 1961-62 SW Georgia Movement violence, three church burnings (Mt. Olive Sasser, Mt. Mary Chickasawatchee, Zion Hope Carlson), and Sheriff Mathews's intimidation campaigns
proposals.transcript_evidence: Transcript: the "Thereocardi" / "Terracalty" / "Tera County" garbles across the entry; the county's Movement significance is the frame for Browner's entire narrative.

Figure / Entity
proposals.why_they_belong: Role
proposals.transcript_evidence: Why corpus-worthy

proposals.name: P7.1
proposals.role: Shirley Sherrod** (Shirley Miller Sherrod)
proposals.why_they_belong: Obama-era USDA Georgia State Director of Rural Development (2009-2010); daughter of Grace Hall Miller; wife of Charles Sherrod; subject of the July 2010 Andrew Breitbart selectively-edited NAACP video controversy
proposals.transcript_evidence: High canonical value: the Breitbart-NAACP controversy is a foundational Obama-era racial-media event with ongoing relevance; the Spooner family's vindication is cross-corpus material; Shirley Sherrod appears as a figure in Charles Sherrod's transcript (#18) and is central to Grace Miller's testimony. The corpus has Charles Sherrod but not Shirley Sherrod as a standalone entry despite her independent canonical significance.

proposals.name: P7.2
proposals.role: Joseph Miller Sr.** (c. 1924–1965)
proposals.why_they_belong: Baker County GA Black landowner and farmer; among the first two Black registered voters in Baker County; shot in the back by white neighbor George Williamson March 8, 1965 over a cattle-ownership dispute; died March 15, 1965; three subsequent all-white-jury acquittals — canonical pre-Voting-Rights-Act voter-registration-retaliation murder
proposals.transcript_evidence: His murder is first-person canonical primary-source material for the Baker County pre-VRA retaliation pattern and is referenced cross-corpus in Charles Sherrod's interview. The murder and its judicial failure are foundational to understanding why the Baker County movement intensified post-1965.

proposals.name: P7.3
proposals.role: Baker County Movement / Baker County GA voter-registration campaign (1958-1965)
proposals.why_they_belong: SNCC Southwest Georgia Project satellite; foundational Deep South pre-VRA Black voter-registration retaliation case; Joshua Williams / Mr. Ackie King / Mr. Benjamin Cunningham 1958 voter-registration cohort whose homes and cars were KKK-bombed
proposals.transcript_evidence: The Baker County movement has no standalone entry in the corpus even though Baker County is the geographic theater for Charles Sherrod (entry #18) and Grace Miller (entry #50) — the two most Baker-County-specific entries in the corpus. The Albany Movement entry covers the broader Albany campaign but not the Baker County satellite specifically.

proposals.name: Role
proposals.role: Foundational US community-oriented primary care physician; co-founder with Dr. Count D. Gibson Jr. of the Tufts-Delta Health Center (Mound Bayou, MS) 1965 and Columbia Point Health Center (Boston) 1965 — the two anchor sites of the US Community Health Center movement; MCHR co-founder 1964; Physicians for Human Rights co-founder 1986; CUNY Sophie Davis School of Biomedical Education founder 1978

proposals.name: Why in corpus
proposals.role: Entry 54 is his transcript; he is the subject of the most correction-dense transcript in the corpus (130+ corrections across Passes 1-4); he is the foundational figure for the US CHC movement, directly relevant to any transcript touching Mound Bayou, OEO, community health, or MCHR

proposals.name: Field
proposals.role: Value

proposals.name: Role
proposals.role: First Black captain of an American merchant marine ship; commanded SS Booker T. Washington (1942-47) with integrated crew; foundational WWII-era racial integration figure in the US merchant marine

proposals.name: Why in corpus
proposals.role: Directly referenced by Geiger as canonical first-person material; the SS Booker T. Washington / Hugh Mulzac cluster appears in this entry with 3+ distinct Whisper rendering variants ("Hugh Mosack," "Hugh Mulzac" alternating); the name disambiguation is high-stakes

proposals.name: Transcript evidence
proposals.role: "the only ship in the American fleet with a black captain Hugh Mulzac who had a master's license for years but until the war never had a ship to command" (corrected transcript)

proposals.name: P1
proposals.role: Lerone Bennett Jr. (1928–2018)
proposals.why_they_belong: Ebony magazine senior editor; author of *Before the Mayflower* (1962)
proposals.transcript_evidence: Foundational Black-history popularizer; his 1962 book is the canonical first text Brown and the Afro-American Association read; *Before the Mayflower* is referenced by name in at least two corpus entries. High cross-corpus recurrence probability. Priority: high.

proposals.name: P2
proposals.role: King Moshoeshoe II of Lesotho (1938–1996)
proposals.why_they_belong: King of Lesotho 1960–90, 1995–96; opposed apartheid
proposals.transcript_evidence: First-person testimony from a US civil-rights activist who attended Brown's MLK memorial service in Maseru 1968; the only CRHP corpus figure with direct royalty contact in a Southern African anti-apartheid context. Priority: medium.

proposals.name: P3
proposals.role: Prime Minister Chief Leabua Jonathan of Lesotho (1914–1987; PM 1965–86)
proposals.why_they_belong: First PM of independent Lesotho; pragmatic accommodationist with apartheid-era South Africa
proposals.transcript_evidence: Paired with Moshoeshoe II in Brown's Lesotho narrative; canonical figure in post-independence Southern African political history; directly named in corrected transcript. Priority: medium.

proposals.name: P7.1
proposals.role: Allard Lowenstein (1929–1980)
proposals.why_they_belong: NSA president, Eleanor Roosevelt protégé, Dump Johnson architect, U.S. Congressman NY-5 1969–71, assassinated by Dennis Sweeney 1980
proposals.transcript_evidence: Corrected transcript: "Allard Lowenstein. Groupies, of course, he was involved in the dump Johnson movement, very close to Kennedy family...he went to University of North Carolina and became the leader of the...National Student Association...went to Yale Law School...Eleanor Roosevelt was one of his...And eventually got elected to Congress before they germinated his district...was tragically killed, assassinated, really by a former student who was mentally ill, named Dennis Sweeney."

proposals.name: P7.2
proposals.role: Willie Ricks / Mukasa Dada (b. 1942)
proposals.why_they_belong: SNCC field secretary, first to publicly lead "Black Power" chants (spring 1966), paired with Carmichael at June 16, 1966 Greenwood MS rally
proposals.transcript_evidence: Corrected transcript: "It was that summer that Stokeley Carmichael and Willie Ricks stayed that in name, right?" (Pass 1 #67.29 / Pass 4 P4.23: "stayed that in name" = "said that" / "coined that name")

proposals.name: P7.3
proposals.role: William "Bill" Webber (1920–2014)
proposals.why_they_belong: Union Theological Seminary professor, founder of East Harlem Protestant Parish (1948), founder of MUST (Metropolitan Urban Service Training) at Union
proposals.transcript_evidence: Corrected transcript: "I took a year out of Union Theological Seminary and worked something called MUST, a Metropolitan Urban Service Training William (Bill) Webber, had set up at a great year."

proposals.role: Candidate
proposals.why_they_belong: Role
proposals.transcript_evidence: Transcript evidence

proposals.name: GT1
proposals.role: Dr. Arenia Cornelia Mallory
proposals.why_they_belong: Saints Industrial Academy & College president (Lexington, Holmes County MS), 1926–1976; Church of God in Christ–affiliated Black women's education leader; Holmes County school board member early 1970s.
proposals.transcript_evidence: Burns's testimony: "There was a leader on there already by the name of Dr. Arenia C. Mallory, the Saints Industrial School name for her." Cross-corpus with Amos C. Brown #2.

proposals.name: GT2
proposals.role: Abe Osheroff
proposals.why_they_belong: Spanish Civil War / Lincoln Brigade veteran; Brooklyn-born carpenter who built the Mileston Community Center in Holmes County MS, summer 1965. Canonical "white Movement volunteer with skills" figure.
proposals.transcript_evidence: Burns: "a new man named Abe and somebody came... Hey, Abe Osheroff, yeah... So Abe came in and they built that... what we call the Mileston Community Center."

proposals.name: GT3
proposals.role: Dr. Demitri B. Shimkin
proposals.why_they_belong: University of Illinois anthropology; led the Holmes County Health Research Program (1969–78) that produced *The Extended Family in Black Societies* (Shimkin, Shimkin, Frate, eds.; Mouton 1978). Documented FSA-land-cooperative economic resilience and child hypertension in post-Voting-Rights-Act Black Mississippi.
proposals.transcript_evidence: Burns (secretary of Mileston Cooperative that funded the program): "the Mileston Cooperative... helped to initiate the Holmes County Health Research Program... Dr. Demitri Shimkin and his associates."

100.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith

proposals.name: Candidate
proposals.role: Role
proposals.why_they_belong: Why They Belong
proposals.transcript_evidence: Transcript Evidence

proposals.name: Raylawni G. Branch (b. 1941)
proposals.role: First Black woman to integrate USM (September 1965); Hattiesburg NAACP youth chapter; first Black woman at Hattiesburg telephone company, Sears, Big Yank; co-organizer of Hattiesburg Head Start via Greyhound-children tactic; first-person witness to Kennard frame-up, Dahmer assassination, Lynd voter-registration obstruction
proposals.why_they_belong: Subject of the corpus's densest single-transcript first-person account of the Hattiesburg movement; the breadth and canonical specificity of her activities make her a primary corpus figure, not a cross-reference
proposals.transcript_evidence: "I was the first person hired at the (Hattiesburg) telephone company... first one hired by series [Sears]... first one hired by Big Yank... I sent a busload of children, the Lady Bird Johnson, to Washington... We got head start after that."

proposals.name: Jeanette Smith (b. January 1, 1940, Soso MS)
proposals.role: Direct descendant of Captain Newton Knight via Newton + Rachel; co-integrator of USM September 1965 alongside Branch; distinctive Reconstruction-descendant + 1965-higher-ed-integration figure
proposals.why_they_belong: The Newton Knight–Rachel descent line that Smith represents is documented in Victoria E. Bynum's *The Free State of Jones* (2001) and the 2016 Gary Ross film; Smith's biographical position unites pre-Civil-War Reconstruction genealogy with 1960s Movement-era integration testimony in a single speaker
proposals.transcript_evidence: "Captain Newton Knight was able to meet my great-great-grandmother Rachel ... He fell in love with her ... He married her, and he had a family by her. And my grandfather was proud of that family."

proposals.name: Gwendolyn Elaine Armstrong
proposals.role: Third member of September 1965 USM integration cohort (alongside Branch and Smith); graduated from Rowan High School same as Branch
proposals.why_they_belong: Canonically paired with Branch in USM integration history; Branch's transcript names her explicitly and contextualizes her as the alphabetically-front person who never shared a class with Branch; presence in multiple corpus entries warrants canonical entry
proposals.transcript_evidence: "Elaine Armstrong. Gwendolyn Elaine Armstrong. ... She wanted to go to school at home because her mother was handicapped. ... She was an A. Armstrong. So she was usually up front in the class."

proposals.name: GTC.1
proposals.role: Elaine Brown (implied, not named)
proposals.why_they_belong: BPP Chairwoman (1974-77), BPP-women's narrative
proposals.transcript_evidence: The David Hilliard sexual-pressure narrative and the United Front Against Fascism women's speech are part of the broader BPP-women's-narrative being recovered through scholarship (Tracye Matthews, etc.). While Elaine Brown is not named in this transcript, the institutional context warrants her corpus presence for cross-entry BPP-women references. *Low priority — she is a well-known figure and may already appear in the corpus or other entries.

proposals.name: GTC.2
proposals.role: Civil Rights Congress (CRC, 1946-1956)
proposals.why_they_belong: CP-affiliated civil liberties organization; publisher of *We Charge Genocide* (1951)
proposals.transcript_evidence: Franklin Alexander served as Oakland CRC director. The CRC is referenced across multiple corpus entries (Roscoe Proctor, W.E.B. Du Bois angle) but may lack a dedicated corpus entry separate from the individual figures. The CRC published *We Charge Genocide* — a petition to the UN linking US lynching to international genocide law — which is a canonical Civil Rights Movement precursor document.

proposals.name: GTC.3
proposals.role: Herbert Aptheker (1915-2003)
proposals.why_they_belong: CP historian, W.E.B. Du Bois literary executor
proposals.transcript_evidence: Referenced across multiple corpus entries as Bettina Aptheker's father and a foundational CP intellectual. Bettina Aptheker is already proposed; Herbert warrants a separate entry for the CP-historiography thread.

Name / Event
proposals.why_they_belong: Role
proposals.transcript_evidence: Transcript evidence

proposals.role: Thomas Walter Gaither
proposals.why_they_belong: CORE field secretary 1960–62; Friendship 9 organizer-trainer (Jan 31, 1961); Freedom Ride co-conceiver (Dec 1960, with Gordon Carey) and scout (May 1961 first leg); Jackson MS post-Ride coordinator; later Slippery Rock University biology faculty (PhD Iowa).
proposals.transcript_evidence: First-person throughout transcript. Canonical primary-source witness for three foundational CORE events.

proposals.role: James T. McCain
proposals.why_they_belong: CORE field secretary, South Carolina and Southeast region; Sumter SC base; Gaither's recruiter and Movement mentor.
proposals.transcript_evidence: "I would consider James T. McCain to have been my mentor in the civil rights movement … the person who persuaded me to attend a session held by CORE."

proposals.role: Gordon Carey
proposals.why_they_belong: CORE field secretary; December 1960 Freedom Ride co-conceiver with Gaither on the train from South Carolina to New York.
proposals.transcript_evidence: "I think Gordon had a copy of Louis Fischer's book about Gandhi … the idea of a Freedom Ride comes up." Pass 4 confirmed interviewer Mosnier's question: "you and Carey communicate this idea to the folks at the CORE National Office."
